title: Coins for the card shop
read: Think of money like the coins you bring to the card shop. A coin by itself is just metal — you can't play it in a battle. It's valuable only because the shop agrees to trade real cards for it.
Money was invented so you don't have to swap your Pikachu directly for a snack and hope the seller wants a Pikachu. It's a tool everyone agrees to accept — a promise we all trust.

quiz.q: Why is using money easier than trading your Pikachu card straight for a snack?
a: You'd have to find someone who wants exactly that card
quiz.why: Money means you never have to find someone who wants exactly what you have — that's why we invented it.

title: Bench some coins
read: A smart trainer doesn't blow every coin at the shop the second they get it. You 'bench' some for later — keep a few in reserve, like holding energy back for a big move.
Grown-ups call this 'pay yourself first': the moment money arrives, set some aside BEFORE you spend the rest. Spending first and saving 'whatever's left' usually leaves nothing on the bench.

quiz.q: 'Pay yourself first' means…
a: Buy yourself a pack every day
Set some aside to save before spending the rest
quiz.why: Save first, spend second — that way the bench actually has coins on it.

title: Staples vs. chase cards
read: Your deck NEEDS its staple cards to even work — the basics that win games. A shiny chase holo is something you WANT: awesome to have, but your deck survives without it.
Money works the same way. Needs are things you must have (food, a place to live). Wants are the fun extras. Knowing which is which is how every smart money choice starts.

quiz.q: Which one is a WANT, not a need?
quiz.why: A chase card is fun but optional — a want. Water and shelter are needs.

title: Packs keep getting pricier
read: Ask an older player what a booster pack used to cost. Cheaper, right? Pack prices creep up a little every year. That slow creep — on everything — is called inflation.

Here's the sneaky part: coins sitting in your piggy bank buy FEWER packs each year, even though the number of coins never changed. That's the #1 reason people invest — to grow money faster than packs (and everything else) get pricier.

quiz.q: Packs cost a bit more each year. Coins sitting still in your piggy bank will…
quiz.why: Inflation means the same coins buy less later — standing still actually loses ground.

quiz.q: What's a good way to beat inflation?
a: Hide all coins in a jar
Grow your money (invest) faster than prices rise
quiz.why: Investing aims to grow your money faster than inflation eats it.

title: Why invest at all?
read: Coins in a jar are safe but barely grow — and packs keep getting pricier. A great card collection, though, can climb in value over the years.

Investing is just that idea with companies: instead of letting coins sit, you turn them into cards (shares of real businesses) that can grow. More risk than a jar — but over many years, owning good 'cards' has been one of the best ways to grow wealth. You're planting a tree, not buying a snack.

quiz.q: The main reason to invest instead of only saving is…
a: To grow money faster than prices rise
quiz.why: Investing puts money to work so it can outgrow inflation — the whole point.

title: Every company is a card
read: Here's the big reveal of this whole game: every company is a Pokémon card. A 'share' (or 'stock') is one slice of that card — buy one and you really own a tiny piece of the real business.
Add an Apple card to your binder and you own a sliver of every iPhone sold. Companies sell these slices so anyone — even a kid — can collect a piece of a business they love.

quiz.q: Buying 1 share of a company is like…
a: Renting the card for a day
Adding a real ownership slice of it to your binder
quiz.why: One share = one real ownership slice of the business, profits included.

title: Why companies print cards
read: To build factories, hire people, or invent new things, a company needs lots of coins. One way to get them: 'print cards' of itself — sell shares to the public.
You hand the company coins; it hands you a card (a share of ownership). It gets to grow, and you share in whatever profits and growth come next. Everybody can win.

quiz.q: Why does a company sell shares (print cards of itself)?
a: To raise coins so it can grow
quiz.why: Selling shares raises money the company uses to grow — and owners share the upside.

title: The giant trading floor
read: The 'stock market' is one enormous card shop where millions of trainers buy and sell company-cards all day. You don't haggle one-on-one at recess — the market quietly matches every buyer with a seller.
Each card has a short nickname called a 'ticker': Apple is AAPL, Nike is NKE, Nintendo's rival Nvidia is NVDA. Type a ticker into Stock-Dex and you're pulling that card the same way the pros do.

quiz.q: The stock market is basically…
a: A giant shop matching card buyers and sellers
quiz.why: It's a huge marketplace where buyers and sellers of company-cards meet.

title: Who sets a card's price?
read: No shopkeeper stamps a price on a card. A card is worth exactly what trainers will pay for it right now — just like in the schoolyard.
When everyone wants a card, buyers bid the price UP. When everyone's dumping it, the price drops. The crowd sets the price, second by second — not the company.

quiz.q: A card's price went up today because…
a: The company mailed out new prices
More trainers wanted to buy than sell
quiz.why: Prices move on supply and demand — the tug-of-war between buyers and sellers.

title: Why a Charizard costs more
read: A Charizard costs more than a common card for two reasons together: few exist (scarce) AND tons of trainers want it (demand). Either one alone isn't enough.
Companies work the same way. There's a limited number of shares, and a business with a rare, hard-to-copy power gets the holographic treatment — special, and pricier.

quiz.q: What makes a card (or a company) command a high price?
a: It's rare AND lots of trainers want it
quiz.why: Scarcity only matters when demand is high too. Rare + wanted = valuable.

title: HP = how big and tough
read: Top-right of every card: HP. Big HP means a big, tough Pokémon that's hard to knock out. On a company-card, HP is the 'market cap' — the company's total size.
High-HP cards are like fully-evolved Pokémon: sturdy, but they grow slowly. Low-HP cards are Basic-stage: small, bumpier, but with the most room to evolve and grow.

quiz.q: A card's HP (market cap) tells you…
a: How big and tough the company is
quiz.why: Market cap = the company's total size. Big HP = a big, established company.

title: Attacks: damage vs. landed damage
read: The 'Revenue Rush' attack shows all the damage on paper — every dollar coming in from sales. Flashy, but the opponent's defense (the company's costs) blocks a lot of it.
'Profit Strike' is the damage that actually LANDS after all costs are paid. A card can have a giant revenue attack but tiny real profit. Profit is the hit that counts.

quiz.q: A lemonade stand 'attacks' for $100 of sales but spends $90 on lemons. The damage that LANDS (profit) is…
quiz.why: Revenue is the $100 on paper; profit is what's left after costs — $10.

title: How much damage gets through
read: Two cards both 'attack' for $100 of sales. One only lands $10 of real profit; the other lands $50. Same attack — wildly different results.
That 'how much actually gets through' is the profit margin. A high margin means the card is brilliant at turning sales into real profit — one of the clearest signs of a strong business.

quiz.q: A company with a HIGH profit margin…
a: Keeps more profit from each sales dollar
quiz.why: Margin is the slice of each sales dollar that becomes profit — higher is stronger.

title: Flip the card: owns vs. owes
read: Every card has a back. Flip it (tap 'Balance sheet') to see what the company OWNS — cash, buildings, gear — versus what it OWES, its debts.
A healthy card owns way more than it owes, like a trainer with a full coin pouch and no IOUs. Owing more than you own is a flashing Weakness if a tough battle comes.

quiz.q: A financially strong company usually…
a: Owns much more than it owes
quiz.why: Owning more than you owe is the sign of a healthy balance sheet.

title: Debt = a heavy retreat cost
read: Debt is borrowed coins the company must pay back. A little is fine — even useful, like spending energy to power up a bigger attack.
But heavy debt is like a huge Retreat Cost: the card gets stuck. The loan payments are due even on a bad turn when sales are down. That's why big debt shows up as a card's Weakness.

quiz.q: Why is too much debt risky for a company?
a: It must be repaid even in bad times
quiz.why: Debt payments don't pause when business is bad — that's the danger of borrowing too much.

title: The Ability nobody can copy
read: See the 'Ability' on a strong card? That's the passive power rivals just can't copy. In investing it's called a 'moat': Coca-Cola's secret recipe and famous name, Disney's beloved characters, Apple's locked-in ecosystem.
A wide moat guards a company's profits the way a castle moat keeps attackers out. Hunting for cards with powerful, copy-proof Abilities is one of the best moves an investor can make.

quiz.q: Which is the strongest Ability (moat)?
a: A logo everyone trusts and can't copy
quiz.why: A brand or edge rivals can't copy protects profits for years — a true moat.

title: Basic-stage vs. fully evolved
read: Some cards are Basic-stage: small, exciting, with huge room to evolve — but their price bounces around a lot. Others are fully evolved tanks: big, steady, often paying dividends, but slower to grow.
Neither is 'better.' Growth cards chase a bigger future; steady cards give reliability and income today. Strong trainers keep some of each on the team.

quiz.q: A young, fast-growing (Basic-stage) company is usually…
a: More exciting but bumpier and riskier
quiz.why: Fast growth comes with bigger price swings and more risk — exciting but bumpy.

title: Rarity = the price tag (P/E)
read: A Secret Rare holo costs way more than a common, even for the same Pokémon. The card's RARITY is its price tag. For companies, that price tag is the P/E ratio — how many coins you pay for $1 of the company's yearly profit.
High P/E = a holographic, premium card: the crowd expects huge future growth. Low P/E = a common: cheaper, calmer hopes. Holo doesn't mean 'better in battle' — it means 'more expensive to buy.'

quiz.q: A company has a P/E of 60 (a 'Secret Rare' price tag). That usually means…
a: Trainers pay a big premium and expect lots of growth
quiz.why: A high P/E is a high price for today's profit — the crowd is betting on big growth.

title: Best card ≠ priciest card
read: This is the secret the whole game turns on: a card's BATTLE STATS (how good the company is) and its SHOP PRICE are two different things.
A glittering holo can be wildly overpriced for how it actually plays. And a plain common can be a hidden powerhouse going cheap. The best buy is a great card AT a fair price — you need BOTH.
So when you size up a card, ask two questions, not one: Is this a strong company? AND is the price fair? That's how champions pick.

quiz.q: You spot an amazing company, but the price is sky-high. A smart trainer…
a: Checks whether the price is fair first
quiz.why: Great card + too-high price can still be a bad buy. Value = quality AND price.

quiz.q: Can a plain, cheap 'common' company beat a famous, expensive 'holo' one?
a: Yes — if you pay a fair price for what you get
quiz.why: Value isn't about sparkle or fame — it's what you get for the price you pay.

title: Why a card's price jumps
read: A card's shop price can leap on hype: a tournament win, a brand-new set, a hot YouTube video. Sometimes a great card dips on a scary rumor; sometimes a weak one spikes on pure buzz.
The lesson: in the SHORT term, hype and headlines shove prices around. In the LONG term, what wins is how the card actually battles — the company's real results. Results win eventually.

quiz.q: Over many YEARS, what matters most to a card's price?
a: How well the company actually performs
quiz.why: Short-term it's hype; long-term it's real results. Results win out over time.

title: Hot market, cold market
read: Sometimes the whole card market runs HOT — everyone buying, prices charging up. That's a 'bull market' (picture a bull's horns going up). Sometimes it runs COLD — everyone selling, prices swiping down. That's a 'bear market.'
Both are totally normal and both always pass. A cold (bear) market feels scary, but it's also when great cards go on sale.

quiz.q: A 'bear market' means card prices are generally…
quiz.why: Bear = falling; bull = rising. Both phases always pass.

title: Glass cannon vs. tank
read: A glass-cannon card hits for huge damage but faints in one blow — big reward, big risk. A tanky card is reliable but slow. That trade-off runs through ALL of investing.

A safe savings jar is a tank: slow, almost never faints. A hot new stock is a glass cannon: could double, could crash. The goal isn't zero risk (too slow) or wild risk (too reckless) — it's SMART risk you understand and can survive.

quiz.q: A card that could earn a LOT usually…
a: Also carries a bigger risk of fainting (losing)
quiz.why: Higher possible reward almost always comes with higher risk — no free lunch.

quiz.q: The smartest approach to risk is to…
a: Bet everything on one wild card
Take risks you understand and can handle
quiz.why: Smart, understood risk is the sweet spot between too-slow and too-reckless.

title: Don't run an all-Fire deck
read: Build a deck of ALL Fire-types and a single Water gym wipes you out. Smart trainers mix types so one bad matchup can't end the whole battle.

Your binder of companies is the same. Spreading across different types — 'diversifying' — means when one card zigs, another zags. Check the colored bars in your portfolio: is one type eating the whole pie?

quiz.q: Why mix different company 'types' in your binder?
a: So one bad matchup can't wipe you out
quiz.why: Diversifying lowers risk — trouble in one type won't sink the whole team.

quiz.q: Your entire binder is one company. That's…
a: Risky — everything rides on one card
quiz.why: All-in on one card means one piece of bad news can hurt everything you own.

title: The price wiggles daily
read: A card's shop price jiggles every single day — up a little, down a little. That wiggling is called volatility, and it's completely normal. Even Charizard has red days.
Big idea: the card didn't get weaker when its price dipped — only the price moved. A drop is just a 'paper loss.' It becomes a REAL loss only if you panic and sell your good card cheap.

quiz.q: Your card's price falls 10%, but you don't sell. You have…
a: A paper loss that isn't real yet
quiz.why: Losses lock in only when you sell. Hold a good card and the dip can recover.

title: You can't call the peak
read: Loads of trainers try to guess the exact day a card will peak so they can sell at the top. The truth? Almost nobody can do it — not even pros. People who flip in and out usually miss the biggest jumps.
Calmly HOLDING good cards over years beats darting in and out. It's 'time IN the market,' not 'timing the market.'

quiz.q: Trying to guess the exact perfect day to buy and sell usually…
a: Fails — holding good cards works better
quiz.why: Timing the market is nearly impossible; patient time IN the market wins.

title: Cards that pay you to bench them
read: Some cards drop a coin into your hand every turn just for keeping them on your bench — that's a dividend. Coca-Cola has paid one for over 60 years straight.
Even better, you can REINVEST those coins to buy more cards automatically — pouring extra fuel on the compounding fire you're about to learn.

quiz.q: What's a dividend?
a: A fee you pay to own a card
A coin the company pays you for holding it
quiz.why: A dividend is the company sharing profit with its owners — income just for holding.

title: Energy that builds on itself
read: In battle, energy builds up turn after turn until you unleash something huge. Compounding is money's version: your gains start earning their OWN gains, snowballing slowly then explosively.
Kids have the one ingredient pros wish they had — time. Open the Time Machine tab and watch a small pile grow over 20 or 30 years. Starting early beats starting big, every single time.

quiz.q: What makes compounding so powerful?
a: You add huge amounts of money
Your gains start earning their own gains, snowballing
quiz.why: Compounding = earnings on your earnings. Given time, the snowball gets enormous.

quiz.q: Who gets the MOST out of compounding?
quiz.why: Time is the secret ingredient — starting early beats starting big.

title: Buy a pack every month
read: Instead of blowing all your birthday money on packs in one day and stressing about timing, you buy one pack every month. Investors call this 'dollar-cost averaging.'
Some months packs are pricey, some months cheap — and it all averages out. It turns the scary question 'is NOW the right time?' into a calm, automatic habit.

quiz.q: Investing a little every month (instead of all at once)…
a: Guarantees you buy at the bottom
Smooths out the ups and downs over time
quiz.why: Buying steadily averages your price and removes the stress of timing.

title: Buy the whole booster box
read: Instead of hunting one perfect card, you could buy a whole booster BOX — a little of everything at once. An 'index fund' is exactly that: one purchase that holds tiny pieces of hundreds of companies.
Buy one and you're instantly diversified, cheaply and easily. Many of the smartest investors alive say simple index funds are the best choice for most people.

quiz.q: An index fund lets you…
a: Own tiny pieces of many companies at once
quiz.why: One index fund = instant diversification across hundreds of companies.

title: The shop takes a cut
read: Every time you trade at the card shop, the shop takes a small cut. Same with investing fees — and they sound tiny, like 1%.
But over 30 or 40 years those little cuts quietly eat a HUGE bite of your final pile. Trading less and choosing cheap, simple funds wins the long race.

quiz.q: Over many years, high fees…
a: Quietly shrink your returns a lot
quiz.why: Small fees compound against you over decades — keep them low.

title: Chase-card fever & panic
read: Two feelings wreck more trainers than any rival. Chase-card fever (FOMO) makes you overpay for whatever's hyped because everyone else is grabbing it. Panic makes you dump your good cards cheap the second prices dip.
Champions notice these feelings and slow down. A handy rule: be a little careful when everyone's greedy, and a little brave when everyone's scared.

quiz.q: Buying a card ONLY because it's soaring and everyone's hyped is driven by…
quiz.why: Chasing hype is FOMO — a classic way to buy high and get hurt.

quiz.q: When everyone around you is panic-selling, a wise trainer often…
quiz.why: Calm beats crowd-panic. Fear is when the big mistakes happen.

title: Beware the fake Charizard
read: Watch out for the kid hissing 'guaranteed mint Charizard, today only, double your money!' Real cards are never guaranteed riches, and the rare ones are often the most faked.

Investing scams use the same tricks: urgency ('act now!'), secret 'can't-lose' tips, promises of fast guaranteed money. The golden rule: if it sounds too good to be true, it is. Protecting your coins matters as much as growing them.

quiz.q: Someone guarantees you'll double your money in a week. You should…
a: Walk away — it's almost certainly a scam
Tell all your friends to join
quiz.why: Guaranteed fast riches = scam. Real investing is slow and never guaranteed.

title: Leave it in the binder
read: A great card left safe in your binder can climb in value for years. One you yank out to trade every week never gets the chance — and the shop nibbles a fee each time.
The best trainers are almost boring: they pick good cards and wait. A seed left in the ground becomes a tree; a seed dug up every week to 'check on it' never grows at all.

quiz.q: The market drops 10% in a scary week. The wise long-term move is usually to…
a: Stay calm and keep holding good cards
quiz.why: Time in the market beats timing the market. Patience usually wins.

title: Build for the right tournament
read: Are you building a deck for next weekend's tournament, or one years away? That answer changes everything about how much risk fits.

Money you'll need very soon should stay safe — a bad week shouldn't be able to wreck it right when you need it. Money for a faraway goal CAN ride out the bumps and aim for more growth. Match the risk to the timeline.

quiz.q: Money you'll need next month should be…
a: In risky cards for max growth
Kept safe, NOT in bumpy stocks
quiz.why: Short-term money stays safe; only money you won't need for years belongs in stocks.
